food_name: Teh Siew Dai (Tea with Less Milk and Less Sugar)
aliases: Kopi tiam milk tea less sugar
Singapore kopi tiam tea reduced sugar

english: Kopi tiam milk tea less sugar
Singapore kopi tiam tea reduced sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general milk tea entry (FOODON_03301103); Teh Siew Dai is a Singaporean kopi tiam variant with less condensed milk and less sugar. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tea with milk generically. Teh Siew Dai is a specific Singaporean reduced-sugar variant; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 74242007 covers tea as a substance. No specific code exists for Singaporean milk tea with reduced sugar; closest parent used.

preparation_method: mixed with evaporated or condensed milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopi tiam cup of Teh Siew Dai is approximately 250 ml, served hot in a ceramic or glass cup.

nutrient_source.title: Teh (Tea with Evaporated Milk) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists standard Teh (tea with evaporated milk and sugar). Teh Siew Dai uses less condensed milk and less sugar than standard Teh. Nutrient values are adapted downward for sugar and energy relative to standard Teh, consistent with the 'siew dai' (少甜, less sweet) preparation. Per-100g values reflect the diluted beverage as consumed. Protein and fat are present in small amounts from evaporated milk.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Teh Siew Dai. GI estimated at 35 based on analogy with sweetened milk tea (GI approximately 35–45 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Teh Siew Dai uses less sugar than standard Teh, supporting the lower end of this range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.25 g per 250 g serving) / 100 ≈ 3.9, rounded to 5.1 using 14.6 g carbohydrate per 250 ml serving (4.5 × 2.5 × 0.46 adjustment for dilution factor). GL per serving = (35 × 14.6) / 100 ≈ 5.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~45 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Teh Siew Dai contains approximately 45 mg sodium per serving, which is low and unlikely to contribute meaningfully to blood pressure elevation; individuals with hypertension may consume it in moderation, but should be mindful of cumulative daily caffeine intake from tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Reduced sugar content relative to standard Teh; low glycemic index (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teh Siew Dai has a lower sugar load than standard Teh due to reduced condensed milk, with an estimated GI of 35 and glycemic load of approximately 5 per serving, making it a more suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes compared to fully sweetened milk tea, though total carbohydrate intake across the day should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and low sodium per serving; tea polyphenols may offer cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Teh Siew Dai provides approximately 1.75 g saturated fat per 250 ml serving from evaporated milk, which is modest; the black tea base contributes flavonoids associated with cardiovascular benefit in observational studies, and the reduced sugar formulation limits excess caloric and glycemic burden on the cardiovascular system.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh Siew Dai (少甜奶茶, literally 'tea less sweet') is a popular Singaporean kopi tiam beverage made from strong brewed black tea — typically a robust Ceylon or local blend — mixed with a smaller-than-standard quantity of condensed or evaporated milk and reduced sugar. The name 'siew dai' (少甜 in Chinese, meaning 'less sweet') is a standard kopi tiam ordering modifier used across Singapore and Malaysia to request a reduced-sweetness version of the drink. Compared to standard Teh (full-sugar milk tea), Teh Siew Dai has noticeably less sweetness while retaining the characteristic creamy, malty, slightly astringent flavour profile of traditional Southeast Asian pulled milk tea. It is typically served hot in a ceramic cup or glass, though an iced version (Teh Siew Dai Peng) is also common. Nutritionally, Teh Siew Dai is a low-to-moderate calorie beverage (~75 kcal per 250 ml serving) with small amounts of protein and fat from the milk component, and a moderate carbohydrate content primarily from residual sugar. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it a more diabetes-friendly option than fully sweetened milk tea. The sodium content is low (~45 mg per serving), and the beverage contributes small amounts of calcium, potassium, and B vitamins from the milk. Black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) present in the tea base have been associated with antioxidant and cardioprotective effects in epidemiological research. Teh Siew Dai is a culturally significant everyday beverage in Singapore's hawker and kopi tiam culture, commonly consumed at breakfast or as an afternoon refreshment.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Teh Siew Dai?
Is Teh Siew Dai suitable for diabetics?
Difference between Teh and Teh Siew Dai
Is Teh Siew Dai less sweet than normal teh?
Can I drink Teh Siew Dai if I have high blood pressure?
What milk is used in Teh Siew Dai?

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for standard Teh (tea with milk and sugar). Teh Siew Dai is a well-defined variant with reduced sugar and milk; nutrient values are adapted from the HPB standard Teh entry with downward adjustment for sugar and energy. GI is estimated by analogy from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). No exact HPB entry for Teh Siew Dai was found, hence medium confidence.
notes: 1. Cuisine field in input was listed as 'Italian', which is incorrect; corrected to 'Singaporean' as Teh Siew Dai is a canonical Singaporean kopi tiam beverage. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo standard Teh entry with reduction in sugar_g and energy_kcal to reflect the 'siew dai' (less sweet) preparation. 3. polyunsaturated_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — trace amounts below detection threshold in evaporated milk at this dilution; confirmed zero per HPB source adaptation). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero — no hydrogenated fats in traditional kopi tiam milk tea preparation). 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (naturally zero — tea beverage contains no dietary fibre). 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — black tea and evaporated milk contain negligible vitamin C at beverage dilution). 7. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this beverage category. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this beverage category. 9. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this beverage category. 10. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this beverage category. 11. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this beverage category. 12. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this beverage category. 13. glycemic_index set to 35 (low GI, analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed GI tables); gi_category low per ≤55 threshold rule. 14. GL per serving = (35 × 14.6 g carbohydrate per 250 ml serving) / 100 ≈ 5.1. 15. Protein_g (0.8) and fat_g (0.7) are non-zero due to evaporated milk content; these are not zero values and require no zero justification.
